chunk_summary: This section provides low-cost Indian meal plans for toddlers, school-age children, and adolescents, emphasizing measurable follow-up goals to address chronic undernutrition and stunting through practical dietary interventions and adherence tracking.
learning_objectives: Understand age-specific meal plans for addressing chronic undernutrition in Indian children.
Learn how to set measurable dietary goals for follow-up and adherence tracking.
Recognize the importance of energy-dense, protein-rich, and iron-containing foods in combating stunting and wasting.
key_facts: Toddlers (12–23 months): Breakfast includes thick porridge/upma with oil/ghee and fruit; lunch includes khichdi/rice with dal, vegetables, and egg/dairy/soy alternative.
School-age children: Breakfast options include poha/upma with peanuts and milk or roti with egg/curd; afterschool snacks should prioritize roasted chana, peanuts, or chikki over packaged snacks.
Adolescents: Pair iron-rich foods with vitamin C sources and prioritize a protein-rich breakfast to improve school function.
Measurable goals include adding one daily protein serving, replacing tea plus biscuits with milk and a home snack, and tracking school meal uptake under PM POSHAN.
Follow-up for stunting without wasting: Schedule every 2–4 weeks initially, then every 6–8 weeks to assess growth velocity.
For wasting with or without stunting: Use higher-frequency follow-up aligned to risk.
reference_quote: ‘Write 3–5 measurable goals such as adding one daily protein serving, replacing tea plus biscuits with milk plus a home snack on school days, and documenting the number of days per week the child receives the school meal under PM POSHAN.’
document_key_topics: Low-cost Indian meal plans for chronic undernutrition
Measurable dietary follow-up goals for stunting and wasting
Age-specific nutrition strategies for toddlers, school-age children, and adolescents
